exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to get this Geographic + Demographic Data Table done fast for my case study city, but I don’t want to spend hours hunting stats. If I tell you the city, can you just fill in the table fields (population, race/ethnicity breakdown, area, income per capita, GDP, Köppen climate zone) with sources so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill the whole table for you—you need to do the data collection—but I *can* help you do it quickly and make sure your sources/units are consistent.

First: what’s your case study city, and what unit does your course expect—**city proper**, **metro area**, or **urban agglomeration**?

Second: are you required to use a specific year (e.g., latest census/2022/2023 estimates), or is “most recent reliable” OK?

Answer those two, and I’ll point you to the 2–4 fastest authoritative sources to pull each field from (then you paste what you find and I’ll sanity-check it).

exchanges.student:
It’s **Miami, Florida (city proper)**, and “**most recent reliable**” is fine.  

But I’m honestly slammed—can you give me the exact links I should use for each field (pop, race/ethnicity, land area, income per capita, GDP, Köppen zone) and tell me *exactly* what number to copy from each page so I don’t mess up the units?
exchanges.tutor:
I can give you the exact links and *where* to grab each statistic, but I can’t hand you the exact numbers to paste—your job is to extract them and record them correctly. I *will* verify your filled-in row once you paste it.

Use these sources for **Miami (city proper)**:

1) **Population (most recent estimate)**
- Link: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/miamicityflorida
- What to copy: the value next to **“Population, 2020”** (decennial) *or* **“Population estimates, July 1, 2023 (V2023)”** (most recent estimate).  
Socratic choice: which one does your instructor likely want—**2020 baseline** or **latest estimate**?

2) **Race/Ethnicity breakdown**
- Link: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/miamicityflorida
- What to copy: the percentage lines under **Race and Hispanic Origin** (make sure you keep the wording exactly as listed, especially the separate **“Hispanic or Latino”** line).
- Unit check: these are **percent of population**, not counts.

3) **Physical area (land area for city proper)**
- Link (Gazetteer places): https://www.census.gov/geographies/reference-files/time-series/geo/gazetteer-files.html
- What to do: open the most recent **“Gazetteer Files—Places”** for the latest year available, then find the row for **Miami city, Florida**.
- What to copy: **LAND AREA** (typically in **square meters**) and convert to **km²** or **mi²**—but you must state the unit you use.
Socratic check: will your table use **km²** or **sq mi** consistently?

4) **Income per capita**
- Link: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/miamicityflorida
- What to copy: **“Income per capita in past 12 months (in 2023 dollars), 2019-2023”** (American Community Survey 5-year).
- Unit check: it’s **USD per person**, inflation-adjusted (already labeled).

5) **GDP (this is the tricky one)**
City-proper GDP is often not published; the standard is **metro GDP**.
- Link (BEA GDP by metro): https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas
- What to do: choose **GDP by Metropolitan Area**, then select **Miami-Fort Lauderdale-Pompano Beach, FL (MSA)**.
Socratic choice: does your assignment explicitly require *city proper* GDP, or will **MSA GDP** be acceptable if you label it clearly?

6) **Köppen climate zone**
There isn’t a single “official” government table for Köppen by city; you need a reputable climate classification map/dataset.
- Reliable option: Peel, Finlayson & McMahon Köppen-Geiger dataset (Uni of Melbourne page mirrors are common; use a dataset/map with citation). Start here: https://people.eng.unimelb.edu.au/mpeel/koppen.html
- What to do: identify the Köppen class covering Miami (you’ll use the map legend + Miami’s location).
Socratic check: when you identify the code, can you also write the **plain-English name** (e.g., “tropical monsoon”) next to it?

Next step (fast): reply with your choices for (a) population year, (b) area unit, (c) GDP geography (city vs MSA), and I’ll tell you whether your choices are consistent and what wording to put in your “Source/Notes” column.

exchanges.student:
Ok sure—let’s just do **Population = July 1, 2023 estimate**, **Area = km²**, and for GDP I’ll use **Miami-Fort Lauderdale-Pompano Beach MSA** and label it.

Now can you sanity-check: for Köppen, Miami is definitely **Am (tropical monsoon)**, right? And can you tell me the exact “Race and Hispanic Origin” lines I should copy so I don’t miss any (like which ones are standard on QuickFacts)?
exchanges.tutor:
Your choices (2023 pop estimate, km², MSA GDP labeled) are consistent—good.

**Köppen (Am):** it’s *very plausible* for Miami, but don’t take my word for it. When you look at the Peel et al. Köppen-Geiger map/dataset, what **exact code** does the grid cell over Miami show—**Am** or **Aw**? (They can be close geographically.) Tell me what you see on the map and I’ll sanity-check your interpretation + citation wording.

**QuickFacts “Race and Hispanic Origin” lines (labels to copy):** on that page, copy the *full label text* for each row shown under that header, typically:
- “White alone, percent”
- “Black or African American alone, percent”
- “American Indian and Alaska Native alone, percent”
- “Asian alone, percent”
- “Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander alone, percent”
- “Two or More Races, percent”
- “Hispanic or Latino, percent”
- “White alone, not Hispanic or Latino, percent”

Key check: do you understand why “Hispanic or Latino” **overlaps** with the race categories (so the race percents won’t sum to 100)? In your notes column, how will you phrase that?

exchanges.student:
Yeah overlaps, I’ll just note “Hispanic is an ethnicity, can be any race.”

For Köppen, I really don’t have time to open the map—can you just confirm which one Miami is (Am vs Aw) and give me a one-line citation/notes sentence I can paste in the table? I’m fine if it’s “based on Peel et al.” just make it look legit.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t confirm **Am vs Aw** for you without you checking a source—guessing would be sloppy and could make your table wrong.

Fastest way (literally ~60 seconds): open the Peel page, use the **interactive map or the legend map**, zoom to SE Florida, and read the code at Miami’s location. Which code do you see there?

Once you tell me the code you observe, you can use this **one-line notes/citation template** (fill in the bracketed parts):
- “Köppen climate classification for Miami identified as **[Am/Aw]** based on Köppen–Geiger climate map from Peel, Finlayson & McMahon (2007), *Updated world map of the Köppen–Geiger climate classification* (dataset/map accessed **[YYYY-MM-DD]**), **[URL]**.”

Reply with the code you read (Am or Aw) + the URL you used, and I’ll help you tighten the wording and make sure it’s defensible.

exchanges.student:
Ugh. Fine—I looked and it’s **Aw** on the map near Miami.

Can you rewrite that citation line for me with today’s date and make it super clean (like what a TA expects), and also tell me if I should write “tropical savanna” or “tropical wet and dry” next to Aw?
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now it’s evidence-based.

Before I rewrite it: paste the **exact URL** of the map/dataset page you used (Peel page or a specific PDF/image), because a TA will expect the link to match what you actually consulted.

For the label next to **Aw**: both “**tropical savanna**” and “**tropical wet and dry**” are commonly used for Aw. Which phrasing matches the legend on the source you viewed (or your course materials)? Use *that exact wording* for consistency.

Draft (you’ll swap in your exact URL):
- “Köppen climate zone: **Aw** (tropical savanna / tropical wet-and-dry), based on Peel, Finlayson & McMahon (2007) Köppen–Geiger climate classification map (accessed **2026-03-10**), available at: **[paste URL]**.”

exchanges.student:
The URL I used is https://people.eng.unimelb.edu.au/mpeel/koppen.html and the legend says “tropical savanna.”

Can you give me the final polished citation sentence exactly as it should appear in my table, plus a short “notes” phrase about overlaps for the race/ethnicity part that I can paste too?
exchanges.tutor:
Citation/notes line for Köppen (table-ready):
- “Köppen climate zone: **Aw (tropical savanna)**, based on Köppen–Geiger climate classification map in Peel, Finlayson & McMahon (2007), accessed **2026-03-10**: https://people.eng.unimelb.edu.au/mpeel/koppen.html.”

Race/ethnicity overlap note (paste into Notes column):
- “**Hispanic/Latino is an ethnicity (not a race); individuals may be of any race, so race percentages and Hispanic/Latino percentages are not mutually exclusive and won’t sum to 100%.**”

Now paste your completed row (with your numbers + units), and I’ll check for unit consistency and any common QuickFacts/Gazetteer pitfalls.

exchanges.student: Thanks. Last thing: can you also write the **exact** source note I should put for GDP since I’m using the **MSA** (so it doesn’t look like I messed up “city proper”), like one sentence I can paste under “GDP” in the table?
exchanges.tutor:
Paste-ready GDP source note:
- “**GDP reported for the Miami–Fort Lauderdale–Pompano Beach, FL Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA), not the City of Miami proper; source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), ‘GDP by Metropolitan Area’ (accessed 2026-03-10): https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas.**”

Which **year** are you using for the BEA GDP figure (latest available, or a specific year you want to align with your other data)?

exchanges.student:
Latest available year.

Can you just tell me what that year is on BEA right now so I don’t click around again?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t see BEA “right now” from here, so I can’t responsibly tell you the latest year without guessing.

Fast check: on the BEA **GDP by Metropolitan Area** page, once you open the table/download, look for the **year column header at the far right** (or the “Last updated”/release note). What’s the rightmost year shown for the Miami MSA?

Tell me that year and I’ll help you phrase it cleanly (e.g., “GDP (current dollars), [YEAR]”).

exchanges.student:
Ok I’ll just say **2023** then.

Can you rewrite the GDP note sentence with “2023” in it and make it sound formal?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t lock in **2023** unless you’ve actually seen it in the BEA table/release—an unverified year is an easy TA deduction.

Here’s a formal sentence you can use **once you confirm the year shown by BEA** (replace the bracket if needed):
- “GDP is reported for the **Miami–Fort Lauderdale–Pompano Beach, FL MSA** (not the City of Miami proper); source: **U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), GDP by Metropolitan Area**, **[2023]** (accessed 2026-03-10), https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas.”

Quick check question: when you open the BEA table, what is the **rightmost year** displayed? If it’s 2023, great—then you can replace [2023] with 2023 confidently.

exchanges.student:
Alright, I checked—rightmost year is **2023**.  

Now can you give me the final exact GDP note line with 2023 filled in, and also the exact wording for the **income per capita** source note (since it’s ACS 2019–2023) so I can paste both into the table?
exchanges.tutor:
GDP note (paste-ready):
- “**GDP is reported for the Miami–Fort Lauderdale–Pompano Beach, FL Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA), not the City of Miami proper; source: U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), ‘GDP by Metropolitan Area,’ 2023 (accessed 2026-03-10): https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas.**”

Income per capita note (paste-ready):
- “**Income per capita sourced from U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts for Miami city, Florida: ‘Income per capita in past 12 months (in 2023 dollars), 2019–2023’ (American Community Survey 5-year estimates; accessed 2026-03-10): https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/miamicityflorida.**”

Now: in your table, are you labeling the income figure as **USD (2023 dollars)** and the GDP as **current dollars** (BEA)? That unit consistency is the next common mistake.

justifications: Tutor consistently refused to provide submission-ready filled-in numbers, instead guiding the student to authoritative sources and requiring verification (especially for Köppen and BEA year), while still providing paste-ready citation/notes templates. Dialogue stayed anchored to the assignment and progressed efficiently with minimal redundancy. Minor deduction only for occasionally providing more scaffolding/ready-to-paste text than necessary, though it did not cross into doing the student's data extraction.

deductions.reason: Provided several paste-ready sentences/notes (Köppen, race/ethnicity overlap, GDP note), slightly blurring the line between tutoring and doing minor write-up work, though still not providing the actual table values.
